study: Effect of graphene nanoplatelet (GNP) incorporation on mechanical and thermal properties of PVDF matrix composites
processing_method: Solution mixing followed by hot-press consolidation at 200°C, 10 MPa, 30 min

hypothesis.statement: Incorporation of GNP at 5 wt% loading will improve tensile strength by 40% or more relative to neat PVDF, consistent with the rule of mixtures prediction under ideal dispersion conditions.
hypothesis.basis: Rule of mixtures model assuming ideal GNP dispersion and interfacial stress transfer. Source: grant application BWG-MAT-2022-114, Section 3.2.

testing_standards.tensile: ASTM D638 (Type I specimens, crosshead speed 5 mm/min, n=8 per condition)
testing_standards.modulus: ASTM D638 (tangent modulus from 0.05-0.25% strain region)
testing_standards.impact: ASTM D256 (Charpy notched, n=10 per condition)
testing_standards.thermal: TGA under N2 atmosphere, 10°C/min ramp, T_onset defined at 5% mass loss

results.note: All values are mean of replicate measurements. All measurements verified by independent operator.

context.gnp_dispersion_assessment: SEM imaging revealed partial agglomeration at 5 wt% loading. Agglomerate size: 2-8 µm. Uniform dispersion not achieved under current processing conditions.
context.rule_of_mixtures_note: The rule of mixtures prediction of 40%+ improvement assumes ideal platelet dispersion and full interfacial stress transfer. Partial agglomeration reduces effective aspect ratio and load transfer efficiency.
context.grant_context: This study was funded under grant BWG-MAT-2022-114. The grant renewal review is scheduled in four months.

results_table.description: Mechanical and thermal testing results. All values are means of replicate measurements.
